title: MCP StdioTransport: stderr not read, 10MB line limit AFTER read_line already allocated, no line-by-line read cap
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

**Dimension:** Security and Access / Error Orchestration

**Location:** /home/doll/OpenClaudia/src/mcp.rs lines 155-175 (spawn), 219-237 (response size check), no stderr handling anywhere.

**Violation:**
1. **Stderr is piped but never read.** Line 159: .stderr(Stdio::piped()). The Child is stored in an Arc<Mutex<Child>> but nothing ever reads child.stderr. On POSIX, when the server writes enough stderr to fill the pipe buffer (~64 KiB), the server BLOCKS on write(2). From there the server cannot respond to stdin requests, the whole MCP session deadlocks, and the 10-minute tokio::time::timeout (if configured) eventually fires.
2. **The 10 MB size cap is applied AFTER read_line has already allocated the line.** Lines 222-235: reader.read_line(&mut buf) is called with NO limit, then buf.len() is checked against MAX_RESPONSE_SIZE. A hostile MCP server can send a single line of 10 GB and OOM the host BEFORE the check fires. BufReader::read_line accepts up to usize::MAX bytes.
3. **No per-request timeout in StdioTransport::request.** The only timeout lives in McpManager::call_tool_with_timeout. Direct StdioTransport::request users (none currently but public API) block forever on a stuck server.
4. **request_id overflow.** request_id: AtomicU64 (line 181) monotonically increases; after 2^64 requests it wraps. In practice not exploitable, but the check at line 242 ('if response.id != id { Protocol error }') becomes invalid if wraparound ever intersects.
5. **McpError::Io exists but is never constructed.** Line 42: #[from] std::io::Error. Every io::Error is currently wrapped via Transport(format!("...")) — structure is lost. (Derives a grep for 'McpError::Io(' yields no constructors.)

**Mandated Refactor:**
1. Spawn a background task at StdioTransport::spawn that reads child.stderr line-by-line into a ring buffer; include the last 4 KiB in any error bubbled from request().
2. Replace read_line with reader.take(MAX_RESPONSE_SIZE as u64).read_line(...) — this bounds memory BEFORE allocation. Verify the response ends with a newline; if take() hit the cap mid-line treat as protocol error.
3. Apply a per-request timeout inside StdioTransport::request via tokio::time::timeout on the read.
4. Add a McpError::Io(io::Error) construction by dropping the format! wrappers; use ? to propagate structured IO errors.
